title: The State of Simulation for Physical AI: An Overview
gist: An NVIDIA-authored survey of the 2026 physical-AI simulation stack argues that GPU-parallel simulation, not internet-scale data, is what will unlock robot learning, and flags Isaac Lab 3.0's decoupling from Omniverse plus the multi-company Newton physics engine as the emerging shared infrastructure layer to watch.

themes.theme: The data bottleneck behind physical AI

insights.claim: Unlike LLMs and VLMs, which can train on internet-scale data, physical AI systems lack an equivalent data source because a robot must directly learn the consequences of interacting with the physical world, such as what happens when a cup slips, a cable bends, or a gripper contacts an object at the wrong angle.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: This is the structural reason robotics has lagged LLM-style scaling, and why simulation infrastructure (not just bigger models) is the current bottleneck to solve.
insights.quote: it needs to understand what happens when a cup slips, a cable bends, or a gripper contacts an object at the wrong angle.

insights.claim: Simulation bridges the physical-AI data gap by letting developers teleoperate robots and scale data collection through GPU parallelism, generating thousands of hours of photorealistic, physically grounded robot experience at a fraction of the cost of real-world data collection.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Cost/speed advantage of simulated data generation is the core economic argument for why simulation vendors and tooling are becoming central to robotics economics.

insights.claim: Simulation's role has shifted from mainly debugging geometry and visualizing robot motion to becoming a core part of the model development loop, now used to generate perception datasets, train reinforcement-learning policies, collect demonstrations, augment real-world data, benchmark models, and test policies against rare or adversarial scenarios.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Explains why industrial labs and academic groups are now building or contributing to simulation engines rather than treating them as a peripheral tool.

themes.theme: Three-computer paradigm for physical AI development

insights.claim: Physical AI development is organized around a three-computer paradigm: a training computer (large GPU cluster for processing data and training foundation models), a simulation computer (GPU workstation/cluster using accelerated physics and RTX rendering to generate robot experience and sensor data), and an on-robot computer (an edge device such as an NVIDIA Jetson AGX Thor-class system that runs the trained policy during deployment).
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: A useful mental model for mapping compute budgets and vendor exposure (training silicon vs. simulation workstations vs. edge inference chips) across the physical-AI stack.

themes.theme: Choosing among a fragmented engine landscape

insights.claim: NVIDIA Isaac Sim, Isaac Lab, MuJoCo, PyBullet, Drake, and Genesis are named as the most popular simulation engines for humanoids and two-arm dexterous manipulation, with each one specialized for a different need such as scaling reinforcement learning, generating synthetic data, or testing algorithms.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete shortlist of the engines an investor or builder should track as the de facto standards for humanoid/manipulation simulation.
insights.locator: Which Simulation Engine Should I Use?

insights.claim: MuJoCo (Multi-Joint dynamics with Contact) is an open-source physics engine built around precise dynamics, contact-rich motion, and model-based optimization, offering a deterministic pipeline, well-defined inverse dynamics with contacts, and strong contact modeling, but it is not always the best choice for photorealistic rendering or massive GPU-parallel simulation.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Clarifies MuJoCo's niche as an accuracy-first tool, implying it is complemented rather than replaced by GPU-parallel engines for large-scale RL.

insights.claim: MuJoCo Warp (MJWarp) is a GPU-accelerated implementation of MuJoCo built in NVIDIA Warp that batches articulated-body, contact-rich physics for many parallel simulated worlds, prioritizing throughput over single-step latency to make large-scale reinforcement-learning policy training more efficient.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Signals the industry direction of porting proven CPU physics engines to GPU-batched execution specifically to serve RL-scale robot training.

insights.claim: Isaac Sim is an open-source robotics simulation framework built on NVIDIA Omniverse and OpenUSD that provides PhysX physics, photorealistic RTX rendering, and sensor simulation for cameras, depth, lidar, radar, and segmentation, and can ingest assets from CAD, URDF, MJCF, USD, and real-world reconstruction pipelines.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Shows Isaac Sim's competitive edge is breadth of asset/sensor support and photorealism, positioning it for digital-twin and perception-data workflows rather than raw RL throughput.

insights.claim: Beyond the leading engines, PyBullet remains a useful CPU baseline for quick prototyping, DART and ODE still serve as Gazebo back-ends, and Drake is positioned as the gold standard for contact-implicit trajectory optimization and rigorous numerics, but the author explicitly says none of these smaller-niche engines can deliver the massive GPU-parallel scale (e.g., thousands of humanoids on one GPU) that large-scale robot learning now requires.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Implies capital and attention in physical-AI tooling will keep concentrating on GPU-parallel engines (Isaac/Newton/MJWarp) rather than legacy CPU-based simulators.
insights.quote: None of them is the answer to “I need 4,096 humanoids on one GPU.”

insights.claim: NVIDIA Isaac Lab 3.0 has decoupled from its Isaac Sim and Omniverse dependency, becoming a lightweight, multi-backend robot-learning framework that lets developers choose Isaac Sim with PhysX and RTX rendering for photorealistic, sensor-rich workflows, or run headless Newton physics for high-throughput simulation, with photoreal sensors available via a standalone OVRTX renderer or the Newton renderer for vision-based RL requiring very large numbers of environments.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: A major architecture change: it decouples the robot-learning API from any single rendering/physics backend, reducing lock-in and letting Newton compete on throughput even within NVIDIA's own stack.
insights.locator: How Isaac Lab 3.0 Relates to Isaac Sim and Newton

themes.theme: Newton: an emerging shared physics layer

insights.claim: Newton is an open-source, GPU-accelerated, differentiable physics engine co-developed by NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, and Disney Research, and is managed through the Linux Foundation; it is built on NVIDIA Warp and OpenUSD and integrates MuJoCo Warp as a key physics backend.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Cross-industry backing (NVIDIA, DeepMind, Disney) plus neutral Linux Foundation governance is a strong non-obvious signal that Newton is being positioned as the shared, vendor-neutral physics standard for embodied AI, not a proprietary NVIDIA lock-in play.

insights.claim: Newton provides multiple distinct solver implementations rather than one universal method: SolverMuJoCo and SolverFeatherstone use generalized coordinates for articulated rigid bodies, SolverSemiImplicit/SolverXPBD/SolverKamino use maximal-coordinate formulations, SolverVBD is an implicit solver for rigid bodies, particles, cloth, and soft bodies, SolverImplicitMPM targets particle-based continuum materials, and SolverStyle3D specializes in cloth simulation.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Reflects a design philosophy of solver-per-physics-regime rather than a single algorithm, which is the mental model needed to pick the right Newton solver for a given robot or material.

insights.claim: The author argues the interesting question for 2026 is no longer which simulation engine is fastest, but which pieces of the simulation stack will become shared infrastructure that different engines build on top of, with the ecosystem fragmenting and recomposing into a layered stack of specializing, interoperating, and sometimes competing tools.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Reframes competitive analysis in robotics simulation away from raw benchmarks and toward which layer (likely physics engines like Newton) becomes the common substrate others depend on.
insights.quote: What we find more interesting in 2026 is a different question: which pieces of the stack are likely to become the shared infrastructure that different engines would build on top of?

insights.claim: A growing share of core physical-AI infrastructure, including physics engines, differentiable simulators, and training pipelines, is being developed in the open with open governance and GPU-accessible tooling, which the author says lowers the barrier to entry and accelerates iteration across the field.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Suggests the competitive moat in physical-AI simulation is shifting away from proprietary engines toward whoever builds the best products and data on top of shared open infrastructure.

insights.claim: Robot simulation is described as having quietly become one of the most important layers in the modern embodied AI stack, since it is where policies are stress-tested, data is amplified, and, increasingly, where entire training pipelines begin.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Positions simulation infrastructure, not just model architecture, as a primary value-creation layer to watch in embodied AI investment and tooling decisions.
insights.quote: Robot simulation has quietly become one of the most important layers in the modern embodied AI stack.

insights.claim: This article is framed as the first in a series: the next installment goes deeper into Warp and MuJoCo Warp specifically, building toward an end-to-end robotics example trained on a GPU using a software stack that the author says did not exist two years ago.
insights.speaker: Johnny Nuñez Cano
insights.so_what: Flags that the underlying GPU robot-learning software stack is extremely new (under two years old), implying the tooling landscape is still immature and likely to keep shifting quickly.
insights.quote: trained on a GPU using a software stack that did not exist two years ago.
insights.locator: Looking Ahead: From Concepts to Practice
